1: הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֶל-בָּבֶל (haddavar asher-dibber YHWH el-Bavel, the word that YHWH spoke concerning Babylon): the opening of the most extensive nations oracle in the OT — two full chapters (50-51). The title formula is emphatic: not through vision or symbolic act but davar (word) — direct divine speech. The word concerning the instrument of judgment is itself a judgment.
2: בֵּל (Bel) and מְרֹדַךְ (Merodakh/Marduk): Bel (='lord') is the title of the chief Babylonian deity Marduk, the patron god of Babylon. הֹבִישׁוּ עֲצַבֶּיהָ (hovish atzabbeha, her idols are put to shame): when Babylon falls, its gods are exposed as powerless. Cf. Isa 46:1-2 (Bel bows; Nebo stoops). The fall of Babylon is simultaneously the defeat of Babylon's gods.
3: מִצָּפוֹן עָלָה עָלֶיהָ גּוֹי (mitzafon alah aleha goy, from the north a nation has come up against her): the standard 'enemy from the north' oracle formula — used for Babylon's attack on Judah in chs 1-6, now applied against Babylon itself. The wheel of judgment turns: the instrument becomes the object.
4: בָּכוֹ יֵלֵכוּ (bakho yelekhu, they will come weeping): Israel and Judah (Israel uYehudah yachdav) will come together — the reunification of the divided kingdom, a theme from chs 30-33. אֶת-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיהֶם יְבַקֵּשׁוּ (et-YHWH Eloheihem yevaqqeshu, they will seek YHWH their God): baqesh (seek earnestly) — the return involves not just physical migration but spiritual re-orientation toward YHWH.
5: בְּרִית עוֹלָם (berit olam, everlasting covenant): the foundational covenant phrase — cf. Gen 9:16 (with Noah), 17:7 (with Abraham), Isa 55:3 (Davidic covenant). The covenant joined at Zion is eternal — not conditional like the Mosaic covenant that was broken (31:32) but permanent like the new covenant (31:33-34). The return from exile culminates in covenant-renewal, not merely political restoration.
6: צֹאן אֹבְדוֹת (tzon ovedot, lost sheep): the lost-sheep metaphor for Israel — used by Jesus directly (Matt 10:6; 15:24; Luke 15:4-6). רֹעֵיהֶם הִתְעוּם (ro'eihem hit'um, their shepherds led them astray): the shepherds (kings and prophets) who led Israel into apostasy. The people have forgotten (shakhechu) their resting place (rivtzam) — the covenant land and its rest.
7: כָּל-מְצָאֵיהֶם אֲכָלוּם (kol-metzaehem akhalum, all who found them devoured them): the nations that conquered Israel claim innocence — 'we are not guilty, for they sinned against YHWH the habitation of righteousness.' The enemies invoke Israel's own covenant guilt as moral justification for predation: the conquerors are YHWH's instruments even while acting from their own interests.
8: נוּדוּ מִתּוֹךְ בָּבֶל (nudu mitokh Bavel, go out from the midst of Babylon): the call to flee Babylon — the foundational text for the eschatological 'come out of her' in Rev 18:4 and 2 Cor 6:17. כָּאֵילִים לִפְנֵי צֹאן (ka'eilim lifnei tzon, like he-goats leading the flock): the goat leads the flock — those who flee first enable others to follow.
9: מֵעַרֵּר גּוֹיִם גְּדֹלִים (me'arrer goyim gedolim, an assembly of great nations from the north): the eschatological coalition against Babylon — historically the Medes (Isa 13:17) and Persians. חִצָּיו כְּגִבּוֹר מַשְׁכִּיל (chitzav kegibor maskil, his arrows like those of a skilled warrior): the arrows return not riqam (empty) — they all find their mark. Total military effectiveness.
10: וְהָיְתָה כַשְׂדִּים לְשָׁלָל (vehayetah Khashdim leshalal, Chaldea will be for plunder): the reversal — Chaldea/Babylon, which plundered all the nations, becomes itself plunder. The retributive principle of 51:24 and Rev 18:6 (repay her double).
11: שֹׁסֵי נַחֲלָתִי (shosei nachalati, plunderers of my inheritance): Israel as YHWH's nachalah (inheritance/portion) — the attack on Israel is an attack on YHWH's own property. כְּעֶגְלָה דָשָׁה (ke'eglah dashah, like a heifer on grass): the leaping/playful image of Babylon's joy over Israel's destruction — frivolous delight in conquest that YHWH will reverse.
12: בֹּשָׁה אִמְּכֶם מְאֹד (boshah imkhem me'od, your mother will be utterly ashamed): Babylon as mother — the city personified as progenitor of its people. אַחֲרִית גּוֹיִם (acharit goyim, the least of nations): the inversion of imperial status — the empire that dominated all nations will be last among them.
13: מֵחֲמַת יְהוָה (mechamat YHWH, because of YHWH's wrath): the explicit theological cause of Babylon's desolation — not geopolitical accident but YHWH's directed wrath. שַׁמָּה תִּהְיֶה לְכֹלָה (shammah tihyeh lekhollah, she will be completely desolate): the superlative desolation formula. Historically ironic: Babylon was indeed abandoned, a desolate mound for centuries.
14: עִרְכוּ עַל-בָּבֶל מִסָּבִיב (irkhu al-Bavel missaviv, take your positions against Babylon from every side): the battle deployment command. כָּל-דֹּרְכֵי קֶשֶׁת (kol-dorkhei qeshet, all who draw the bow): archers surround the city. אַל-תַּחְמֹלוּ (al-tachmolu, do not spare your arrows): no quarter — the covenant standard of cherem applied to Babylon.
15: נָתְנָה יָדָהּ (natneah yadah, she has surrendered): the surrender gesture — offering the hand. נִקְמַת יְהוָה (niqmat YHWH, the vengeance of YHWH): naqam (vengeance/retribution) — YHWH's retributive justice on behalf of his people. The vengeance is not personal spite but covenant-legal satisfaction.
16: כִּרְתוּ זוֹרֵעַ מִבָּבֶל (kirtu zore'a mibbavel, cut off the sower from Babylon): the agricultural infrastructure destroyed. The sword drives everyone to their own people (el-ammo yifanu) — the dissolution of Babylon's polyglot population that sustained its imperial economy (cf. Rev 18:17-19 on the merchants fleeing).
17: שֶׂה פְזוּרָה יִשְׂרָאֵל (seh pezurah Yisrael, Israel is a scattered sheep): pazar (scatter) is the diaspora verb. Two predators named: the king of Assyria first (rishonah) devoured, and last (ve'acharonah) Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon. The two-stage scattering maps onto Assyrian conquest of the north (722 BCE) and Babylonian conquest of the south (586 BCE).
18: לָכֵן כֹּה-אָמַר יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת (lakhen koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot, therefore thus says YHWH of Hosts): the consequential conjunction. YHWH will punish (paqad) the king of Babylon as he punished the king of Assyria. The same judicial standard applies to each imperial power in sequence — precedent binds YHWH's judgment.
19: וַהֲשִׁבֹתִי אֶת-יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-נָוֵהוּ (vahashivoti et-Yisrael el-naveihu, I will restore Israel to his pasture): shuv (return/restore) and naveh (pasture/home) — restoration to covenant land for grazing. כַּרְמֶל וְהַבָּשָׁן (Karmel vehaBashan): the most fertile grazing territories — Carmel and Bashan were proverbial for lush vegetation and well-fed livestock.
20: יְבֻקַּשׁ אֶת-עֲוֹן יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאֵינֶנּוּ (yevuqqash et-avon Yisrael ve'einennu, Israel's iniquity will be searched for and not found): the forensic search — baqash (seek/search diligently) — finding nothing. The complete erasure of recorded iniquity: the covenant-accusation against Israel will have no evidence. This is the experiential content of the new covenant's 'I will remember their sin no more' (31:34).
21: מְרָתַיִם (Meratayim): a cryptographic name for Babylon (from marah = bitter/rebel; meratayim = double rebellion). פְּקוֹד (Pekod): another Babylonian name with wordplay — paqad means 'visit in judgment.' Both names encode YHWH's accusation: Babylon is doubly rebellious and is being visited with judgment. The wordplay is both political geography and theological verdict.
22: קוֹל מִלְחָמָה בָּאָרֶץ (qol milchama ba'aretz, the sound of battle in the land): a terse proclamation — two words frame the announcement. וְשֶׁבֶר גָּדוֹל (veshever gadol, and great destruction): shever (breaking/destruction) is the covenant word for catastrophic collapse (cf. Isa 30:14; Jer 6:14: 'they have healed the wound of my people lightly'). Now there is no light healing.
23: פַּטִּישׁ כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (pattish kol-ha'aretz, the hammer of the whole earth): Babylon's imperial self-designation — the hammer that smashes other nations. אֵיךְ נִכְרַת (eikh nikkrat, how it is cut down!): the elegiac eikh as for Damascus (49:25). The great instrument of divine judgment has itself been broken: the hammer becomes the nail.
24: יָקַשְׁתִּי לְךָ (yaqashti lekha, I laid a snare for you): YHWH as the hunter who set the trap. וְלֹא יָדָעַתְּ (velo yada'at, and you did not know it): the ignorance of the prey — Babylon did not know when the trap was set. כִּי בַיהוָה הִתְגָּרִית (ki vaYHWH hitgarit, for against YHWH you have contended): the root charge — Babylon's arrogance was ultimately a contest (garah) with YHWH himself.
25: פָּתַח יְהוָה אֶת-אוֹצָרוֹ (patach YHWH et-otzaro, YHWH has opened his armory): YHWH's weapons-treasury — the storehouse of covenant judgment. מְלֶאכֶת אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה צְבָאוֹת (melekhet Adonai YHWH Tzvaot, the work of the Lord YHWH of Hosts): Babylon's destruction is described as YHWH's own 'work' — cf. Isa 28:21 where YHWH's 'strange work' of judgment is his own purposeful project.
26: כִּי-עֵת הִיא (ki-et hi', for it is the time): the appointed time (et) — the kairos moment of Babylon's judgment. כְּמוֹ עֲרֵמִים הַחֲרִימוּהָ (kemo aremim hacheriumuha, pile her up like heaps): the grain-heap imagery. Hacharim (completely destroy): the covenant cherem term — total dedication to destruction, the same language used for Canaanite cities in the conquest.
27: טִבְחוּ כָל-פָּרֶיהָ (tivkhu kol-pareha, slaughter all her bulls): the temple sacrifice vocabulary — the Babylonian military/elite as bulls going to slaughter. כִּי-בָא יוֹמָם עֵת פְּקֻדָּתָם (ki-va yomam et pequddatam, for their day has come, the time of their judgment): the yom paquddah (day of visitation/judgment) — the appointed accountability day has arrived.
28: קוֹל נָסִים וּפְלֵטִים (qol nasim ufletim, the voice of those who flee and escape): the news is carried out of Babylon by its survivors. לְהַגִּיד בְּצִיּוֹן אֶת-נִקְמַת יְהוָה (lehagid beTziyyon et-niqmat YHWH, to declare in Zion the vengeance of YHWH): the refugees become heralds in Zion — the crimes committed against YHWH's house are publicly avenged. The proclamation in Zion completes the legal circle.
29: שַׁלְּמוּ-לָהּ כְּפָעֳלָהּ (shalemu-lah kefo'alah, repay her according to her work): the shillem (repay/requite) principle of covenant justice — what Babylon did, do to her. כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר עָשְׂתָה עֲשׂוּ-לָהּ (kekhol asher astha asuha, according to all that she did, do to her): the lex talionis at the national level. Rev 18:6 quotes this principle directly for the eschatological Babylon.
30: לָכֵן יִפְּלוּ בַחוּרֶיהָ בִּרְחֹבֹתֶיהָ (lakhen yippelu vachehurehah birkhovoteiha, therefore her young men will fall in her streets): the lament-pattern — the city's vitality dies in its own public spaces. The same formula applied to Damascus (49:26) and Jerusalem in Lamentations is now applied to Babylon — all proud cities receive the same covenant reckoning.
31: הִנְנִי אֵלֶיךָ זָדוֹן (hineni elekha zadon, behold, I am against you, O arrogance!): the divine hineni elekha formula — used against Tyre, Egypt, and Gog in Ezekiel. Babylon is personified as zadon (pride/arrogance) itself. כִּי בָא יוֹמֶךָ (ki va yomekha, for your day has come): the day of YHWH vocabulary applied to Babylon — cf. Isa 13:6,9.
32: וְכָשַׁל זָדוֹן וְנָפַל (vekhushal zadon venafal, arrogance will stumble and fall): the Proverbs principle at the national level (Prov 16:18: pride goes before a fall). וְהִצַּתִּי-אֵשׁ בְּעָרָיו (vehitzatti-esh be'arav, I will set fire to his cities): the fire-judgment formula visited on the hammer that wielded fire against others.
33: עֲשׁוּקִים בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל וּבְנֵי-יְהוּדָה יַחְדָּו (ashuqim benei-Yisrael uvenei-Yehudah yachdav, the people of Israel and the people of Judah are oppressed together): the reunification of north and south even in their oppression — yachdav (together) prepares for common restoration. מֵאֲנוּ לְשַׁלְּחָם (me'anu leshallecham, they refused to let them go): the Exodus vocabulary — Pharaoh refused (maen) to let Israel go; now Babylon repeats the pattern and faces the same pattern's judgment.
34: גֹּאֲלָם חָזָק (go'alam chazaq, their Redeemer is strong/mighty): the go'el (kinsman-redeemer) theology — the legal-familial concept of the nearest relative with both the obligation and the right to redeem a family member from slavery. YHWH Tzvaot shemo (YHWH of Hosts is his name): the title emphasizes military power — the Redeemer is not only legally entitled but militarily capable. The go'el theme runs from Ruth through Isaiah 40-55 (where 'Redeemer' appears 13 times) to Christ who ransoms.
35: חֶרֶב אֶל-כַּשְׂדִּים (cherev el-Kashdim, a sword against the Chaldeans): the extended sword-sequence poem — nine cherev el (sword against) stanzas using anaphoric accumulation. The sword Babylon wielded against others is now turned against every class of Babylonian society: officials, sages, warriors, horses, foreigners, treasures.
36: חֶרֶב אֶל-הַבַּדִּים (cherev el-habaddim, a sword against her diviners/false prophets): baddim can mean liars/boasters or diviners who speak falsely — both meanings fit. וְנֹאָלוּ (veno'alu, they will become fools): the reversal of Babylon's claim to wisdom and divinatory expertise.
37: חֶרֶב אֶל-הַסּוּסִים (cherev el-hasusim, a sword against her horses): the complete military-economic complex dismantled — horses, chariots (rekev), foreign troops (erev). וְהָיוּ לְנָשִׁים (vehayou lenashim, they will become as women): the covenant-curse reversal of martial valor (Deut 28:25). The treasury (otzarotehah) plundered: economic infrastructure follows the military.
38: חֹרֶב אֶל-מֵימֶיהָ (chorev el-meimehah, a drought against her waters): the wordplay shifts from cherev (sword) to chorev (drought/dryness) — nearly identical sounds (ch-r-v). Babylon's hydraulic engineering (the Euphrates, Tigris, irrigation canals) targeted. אֶרֶץ פְּסִילִים הִיא (eretz pesilim hi', for it is a land of idols): Babylon's water-wealth has become spiritually insane (yiththolellu) through idol-madness — cf. Isa 44:9-20 on the idolater's derangement.
39: צִיִּים וְאִיִּים (tziyyim ve'iyyim, wild desert creatures and jackals): the desolation fauna who occupy ruin sites. This desolation formula applied to Edom (49:33) anticipates Revelation's 'Babylon ... a haunt of every foul bird and beast' (Rev 18:2). The sequence: Sodom → Edom → Babylon → eschatological Babylon, all described in the same vocabulary.
40: כְּמַהְפֵּכַת אֱלֹהִים אֶת-סְדֹם וְאֶת-עֲמֹרָה (kemahpekat Elohim et-Sedom ve'et-Amorah, as when God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah): the identical formula used for Edom (49:18). Edom and Babylon are parallel types of YHWH's total judgment. In the NT, both serve as figures for eschatological judgment (Rev 14:8; 18:2,21).
41: הִנֵּה עַם בָּא מִצָּפוֹן (hineh am ba mitzafon, behold, a people is coming from the north): the enemy-from-the-north oracle pattern used against Judah (1:14; 4:6; 6:1,22) reversed and applied to Babylon. גּוֹי גָּדוֹל וּמְלָכִים רַבִּים (goi gadol umelakhim rabbim, a great nation and many kings): the Medo-Persian coalition under Cyrus.
42: קֶשֶׁת וְכִידוֹן (qeshet vekidon, bow and spear): the full weapons complement. אַכְזָרִי הוּא וְלֹא יְרַחֵמוּ (akhzari hu velo yerachemu, they are cruel and show no mercy): the same characterization given to Babylon when it attacked Judah (6:23) — now applied to Babylon's attackers. The judgment is symmetrical.
43: שָׁמַע מֶלֶךְ-בָּבֶל אֶת-שִׁמְעָם (shama melekh-Bavel et-shim'am, the king of Babylon heard the report of them): the same shama' (hear/listen) that marks Israel's failure throughout Jeremiah — now the king of Babylon hears and his hands go limp (raffu yadav). The word that Babylon refused to hear (YHWH's prophetic warnings through Jeremiah) now comes as military intelligence: the same paralysis that fell on Judah falls on Babylon.
44: כַּאֲרִי יַעֲלֶה מִגְּאוֹן הַיַּרְדֵּן (ka'ari ya'aleh migge'on haYarden): the lion-from-Jordan simile applied verbatim to Babylon as Jeremiah applied it to Edom (49:19). The repetition is deliberate — Edom and Babylon are parallel targets, and YHWH's incomparability (mi khamoni) governs both judgments equally.
45: שִׁמְעוּ עֲצַת יְהוָה (shim'u atzat YHWH, hear the plan of YHWH): the etzah (plan/counsel) of YHWH against Babylon — parallel to 49:20 for Edom. אֲשֶׁר יָעַץ אֶל-אֶרֶץ כַּשְׂדִּים (asher ya'atz el-eretz Kashdim, which he has devised against the land of the Chaldeans): ya'atz (devise/plan/purpose) — YHWH's purposes for the nations are not spontaneous but deliberately planned in his council.
46: מִקּוֹל תִּפְלַשׂ בָּבֶל (miqqol tiplas Bavel, at the sound of Babylon's capture): the earth quakes (ra'ashah ha'aretz) — cosmic-scale seismic response to the fall of the world-empire. בַּגּוֹיִם יִשָּׁמַע זַעֲקָהּ (baggoyim yishama za'aqah, her cry will be heard among the nations): cf. Rev 18:9-10 where the kings of the earth weep at Babylon's destruction from afar. The chapter closes with the full extent of Babylon's significance: its fall reverberates through all the nations, as its rise did.
